title: Reduced GCS — Approach
oneLiner: A medical emergency requiring immediate ABCDE assessment to identify and treat reversible causes (hypoglycaemia, opioid overdose, structural lesion) before irreversible brain injury.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Hypoglycaemia** — single finding: fingerstick glucose <3 mmol/L; treat immediately with 50 mL 50% dextrose IV (or 1 mg glucagon IM). Delayed treatment causes permanent neurological damage.
- **Opioid overdose** — single finding: respiratory rate <8 + pinpoint pupils; give naloxone 400-800 mcg IV, repeat as needed. Can kill within minutes if unrecognised.
- **Intracranial haemorrhage** — single finding: hypertension + bradycardia + irregular respirations (Cushing’s reflex) ± focal neurology. Requires urgent CT head and neurosurgical referral.
- **Meningitis / encephalitis** — single finding: neck stiffness + fever + petechial/purpuric rash. Start IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone) and dexamethasone before CT if no delay.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** decreased consciousness (AVPU or GCS), confusion, unresponsiveness, altered breathing pattern (Cheyne-Stokes, ataxic), focal deficits (hemiparesis, gaze palsy)
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - GCS score (eye, verbal, motor)  
  - Pupils: size, symmetry, light reactivity  
  - Motor response: localising, withdrawing, posturing  
  - Signs of raised ICP (papilloedema, Cushing’s reflex)  
  - Signs of meningism (Kernig’s, Brudzinski’s)  
  - Skin: rash, needle marks, trauma  
  - Odour (alcohol, ketones)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely -> less likely:**
  - **Metabolic:** hypoglycaemia (rapid onset, diaphoresis, tremor), hyponatraemia, hypercapnoea (COPD), uraemia, hepatic encephalopathy, hypoxia, sepsis
  - **Toxic:** opioids (pinpoint pupils, respiratory depression), benzodiazepines, alcohol, GHB, anticholinergics, carbon monoxide
  - **Structural:** intracranial haemorrhage (sudden onset, focal signs), ischaemic stroke, traumatic brain injury, tumour, hydrocephalus, seizure/post-ictal
  - **Infective:** meningitis, encephalitis, cerebral abscess (fever, focal signs, headache)
  - **Psychiatric:** pseudocoma (no organic signs, inconsistent exam, normal vital signs)
  - **Other:** posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES), hypertensive encephalopathy, non-convulsive status epilepticus

- **Splitter:** Unilateral pupillary dilation + contralateral hemiparesis → structural (ipsilateral herniation) vs symmetrical pupils + no focal signs → metabolic / toxic cause. Bedside bedside glucose immediately splits hypoglycaemia from others.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Fingerstick glucose (priority #1)
  - Capillary ketones
  - Pupillary exam
  - AVPU + GCS chart
  - Pulse oximetry, ECG, blood pressure
  - Urine toxicology screen (if available)
- **Bloods:**
  - VBG: pH, pCO2, lactate, glucose, electrolytes
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, calcium, magnesium, phosphate, INR/APTT, troponin, TSH
  - Blood cultures (if febrile)
  - Paracetamol and salicylate levels (if overdose suspected)
  - ABG if hypoxia or respiratory failure
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT head (non-contrast) **urgently** if any of: GCS <13 at any time, focal neurology, seizure, obvious trauma, anticoagulation, age >65, vomiting, amnesia >30 min
  - **Second-line:** CT pulmonary angiogram (if PE suspected), CT venogram (if cerebral venous sinus thrombosis), MRI brain (if encephalitis, posterior fossa lesion)
- **Special tests:**
  - EEG (if non-convulsive status epilepticus suspected)
  - Lumbar puncture (if meningitis/encephalitis suspected) – **must** do CT head first if any contraindication of raised ICP (focal signs, papilloedema, GCS <13, seizure, immunocompromised, anticoagulation)
- **Never-miss test:** **Fingerstick glucose.** A negative result (normal glucose) is the single most common omission that leads to irreversible brain injury. Do it immediately on every patient with reduced GCS.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **ABCDE:**
    - **A:** Assess airway patency – if GCS ≤8 or unable to protect airway, call anaesthetics for intubation
    - **B:** High-flow O₂, target SpO₂ >94%
    - **C:** IV access (2 large bore), fluid resuscitation if hypotensive, bloods, ECG
    - **D:** GCS, pupils, formal neuro exam
    - **E:** Expose, temperature, look for rash, trauma, infection
  - **Treat reversible causes immediately:**
    - Hypoglycaemia: 50 mL 50% dextrose IV or 1 mg glucagon IM (if no IV access)
    - Opioid overdose: naloxone 400-800 mcg IV, repeat 2 min later if no response
    - Seizures: lorazepam 4 mg IV (or diazepam), then levetiracetam [check local policy]
    - Anticoagulation reversal: prothrombin complex concentrate + vitamin K for warfarin-associated ICH; specific reversal for DOACs
    - Raised ICP: head up 30°, hyperventilation (PaCO2 ~4.0‑4.5 kPa), mannitol [check local policy]
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit to HDU/ICU if GCS <9 or unstable
  - Neuro-observations q1h (GCS, pupils, limb power) – escalate if drop ≥2 points
  - Maintain MAP >80 mmHg (to ensure cerebral perfusion)
  - Treat underlying cause: antibiotics for meningitis, dialysis for uraemia, removal of toxin, definitive management of stroke/ICH
  - Manage agitation or withdrawal syndromes (e.g. alcohol withdrawal – CIWA-Ar protocol)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Rehabilitation (speech, physio, OT)
  - Antiepileptics if seizure occurred
  - Neuropsychiatric follow-up if cognitive impairment persists
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride at 1 mL/kg/h (unless hyponatraemia – use isotonic fluids only)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) – **avoid** if ICH or high bleeding risk
  - **Diet/NBM:** keep NBM until GCS ≥15 and swallowing assessed (risk of aspiration)
  - **Observations:** neuro obs q1h + NEWS q30 min initially, then reduce if stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Dextrose 50%:** 50 mL IV over 1‑2 min (for hypoglycaemia). Extravasation causes tissue necrosis – confirm IV patency.
- **Glucagon 1 mg IM** (if no IV access) – short duration; must give oral/IV glucose after.
- **Naloxone:** 400‑800 mcg IV, repeat up to 2 mg if no response. Rebound opioid toxicity (half-life 30-80 min) – monitor for recurrence.
- **Lorazepam:** 4 mg IV slow (over 1 min) – used for seizure. Avoid in severe respiratory depression.
- **Levetiracetam:** 1000‑2000 mg IV over 15 min (for status epilepticus) – [check local policy for exact dose].
- **Common alternatives:** Diazepam (rectal/IV), phenytoin (slower onset).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:** Naloxone may cause acute withdrawal (pain, vomiting, ventricular arrhythmias). Dextrose 50% contraindicated in hyperglycaemic hyperosmolar state (use normal saline instead).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Levetiracetam dose reduce in renal impairment (eGFR <50). Naloxone no adjustment. Dextrose no adjustment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - Giving naloxone 2 mg IV bolus in a rapid sequence can lead to pulmonary oedema and severe withdrawal. **Give small titrated doses (400 mcg) and reassess.**
  - Giving dextrose 50% without confirming hypoglycaemia in a stroke patient may worsen cerebral oedema. **Always check fingerstick first.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** aspiration pneumonitis, airway obstruction, raised ICP → herniation, seizures, cardiac arrythmias (tricyclic overdose), respiratory failure, respiratory paralysis (opioid overdose – naloxone wears off before opioid)
- **Chronic:** persistent cognitive deficit, post‑hypoxic myoclonus, epilepsy, contractures, pressure sores, DVT, C. diff colitis (if prolonged antibiotics)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Hypoglycaemia:** complete recovery if treated within minutes of onset; prolonged (<1 hour) may cause permanent brain damage or death.
- **Structural lesion:** poor if fixed dilated pupils, extensor posturing, or GCS ≤4 on arrival (in‑hospital mortality >70% for ICH with GCS <8).
- **Metabolic/Toxic:** generally reversible with correction of underlying cause, except severe hypoxic‑ischaemic encephalopathy (e.g. post‑arrest).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **DO check pupil reactivity first** – asymmetrical or non‑reacting pupils indicate structural pathology; symmetrical reactive pupils favour metabolic/toxic.
- **DO use AVPU as a rapid triage tool** – P (pain) or U (unresponsive) triggers immediate ABCDE.
- **DO get a collateral history** – from family, GP, ambulance crew: time of onset, medications, trauma, illicit drugs, alcohol, past medical history (especially diabetes, epilepsy, anticoagulation).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **DO NOT assume alcohol intoxication** without checking glucose, toxicology, and CT head – many structural lesions mimic intoxication.
- **DO NOT give naloxone rapidly or in large doses** – can cause acute withdrawal with hypertension, pulmonary oedema, and vomiting.
- **DO NOT forget non‑convulsive status epilepticus** in a persistent reduced GCS with no seizures – consider urgent EEG if GCS not improving.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** GCS worsens despite reversal (e.g. hypoglycaemia – may recur due to insulin overdose); new focal weakness, headache, or confusion; seizure; vomiting.
- **Follow-up:** If cause was hypoglycaemia, ensure patient eats carbohydrate, check capillary glucose q15 min until stable >4.0 mmol/L.  Arrange blood glucose monitor + endocrine referral if unexplained. For overdose reversal, observe for 4-6 hours (naloxone short duration).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg / consultant) immediately if:**
  - GCS drops by ≥2 points
  - GCS ≤8 (consider intubation + ICU)
  - New focal neurology (hemiparesis, dysphasia, facial droop)
  - Signs of raised ICP (Cushing’s reflex)
  - Active seizure >5 minutes (status epilepticus)
  - National Early Warning Score (NEWS2) ≥5
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - Airway compromise (gag reflex absent, excessive secretions)
  - GCS ≤8 with no improvement after initial resuscitation
  - Respiratory failure (PaO2 <8 kPa on 60% O₂, PaCO2 >6.5 kPa)
  - Persistent tachyarrhythmia or cardiac arrest

rawMarkdown:
# Reduced GCS — Approach

**One-liner:** A medical emergency requiring immediate ABCDE assessment to identify and treat reversible causes (hypoglycaemia, opioid overdose, structural lesion) before irreversible brain injury.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Hypoglycaemia** — single finding: fingerstick glucose <3 mmol/L; treat immediately with 50 mL 50% dextrose IV (or 1 mg glucagon IM). Delayed treatment causes permanent neurological damage.
- **Opioid overdose** — single finding: respiratory rate <8 + pinpoint pupils; give naloxone 400-800 mcg IV, repeat as needed. Can kill within minutes if unrecognised.
- **Intracranial haemorrhage** — single finding: hypertension + bradycardia + irregular respirations (Cushing’s reflex) ± focal neurology. Requires urgent CT head and neurosurgical referral.
- **Meningitis / encephalitis** — single finding: neck stiffness + fever + petechial/purpuric rash. Start IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone) and dexamethasone before CT if no delay.

### Presentation

- **Cardinal symptoms:** decreased consciousness (AVPU or GCS), confusion, unresponsiveness, altered breathing pattern (Cheyne-Stokes, ataxic), focal deficits (hemiparesis, gaze palsy)
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - GCS score (eye, verbal, motor)  
  - Pupils: size, symmetry, light reactivity  
  - Motor response: localising, withdrawing, posturing  
  - Signs of raised ICP (papilloedema, Cushing’s reflex)  
  - Signs of meningism (Kernig’s, Brudzinski’s)  
  - Skin: rash, needle marks, trauma  
  - Odour (alcohol, ketones)  

### Differential Diagnosis

- **Most likely -> less likely:**
  - **Metabolic:** hypoglycaemia (rapid onset, diaphoresis, tremor), hyponatraemia, hypercapnoea (COPD), uraemia, hepatic encephalopathy, hypoxia, sepsis
  - **Toxic:** opioids (pinpoint pupils, respiratory depression), benzodiazepines, alcohol, GHB, anticholinergics, carbon monoxide
  - **Structural:** intracranial haemorrhage (sudden onset, focal signs), ischaemic stroke, traumatic brain injury, tumour, hydrocephalus, seizure/post-ictal
  - **Infective:** meningitis, encephalitis, cerebral abscess (fever, focal signs, headache)
  - **Psychiatric:** pseudocoma (no organic signs, inconsistent exam, normal vital signs)
  - **Other:** posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES), hypertensive encephalopathy, non-convulsive status epilepticus

- **Splitter:** Unilateral pupillary dilation + contralateral hemiparesis → structural (ipsilateral herniation) vs symmetrical pupils + no focal signs → metabolic / toxic cause. Bedside bedside glucose immediately splits hypoglycaemia from others.

### Investigations

- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Fingerstick glucose (priority #1)
  - Capillary ketones
  - Pupillary exam
  - AVPU + GCS chart
  - Pulse oximetry, ECG, blood pressure
  - Urine toxicology screen (if available)
- **Bloods:**
  - VBG: pH, pCO2, lactate, glucose, electrolytes
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, calcium, magnesium, phosphate, INR/APTT, troponin, TSH
  - Blood cultures (if febrile)
  - Paracetamol and salicylate levels (if overdose suspected)
  - ABG if hypoxia or respiratory failure
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT head (non-contrast) **urgently** if any of: GCS <13 at any time, focal neurology, seizure, obvious trauma, anticoagulation, age >65, vomiting, amnesia >30 min
  - **Second-line:** CT pulmonary angiogram (if PE suspected), CT venogram (if cerebral venous sinus thrombosis), MRI brain (if encephalitis, posterior fossa lesion)
- **Special tests:**
  - EEG (if non-convulsive status epilepticus suspected)
  - Lumbar puncture (if meningitis/encephalitis suspected) – **must** do CT head first if any contraindication of raised ICP (focal signs, papilloedema, GCS <13, seizure, immunocompromised, anticoagulation)
- **Never-miss test:** **Fingerstick glucose.** A negative result (normal glucose) is the single most common omission that leads to irreversible brain injury. Do it immediately on every patient with reduced GCS.

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **ABCDE:**
    - **A:** Assess airway patency – if GCS ≤8 or unable to protect airway, call anaesthetics for intubation
    - **B:** High-flow O₂, target SpO₂ >94%
    - **C:** IV access (2 large bore), fluid resuscitation if hypotensive, bloods, ECG
    - **D:** GCS, pupils, formal neuro exam
    - **E:** Expose, temperature, look for rash, trauma, infection
  - **Treat reversible causes immediately:**
    - Hypoglycaemia: 50 mL 50% dextrose IV or 1 mg glucagon IM (if no IV access)
    - Opioid overdose: naloxone 400-800 mcg IV, repeat 2 min later if no response
    - Seizures: lorazepam 4 mg IV (or diazepam), then levetiracetam [check local policy]
    - Anticoagulation reversal: prothrombin complex concentrate + vitamin K for warfarin-associated ICH; specific reversal for DOACs
    - Raised ICP: head up 30°, hyperventilation (PaCO2 ~4.0‑4.5 kPa), mannitol [check local policy]
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit to HDU/ICU if GCS <9 or unstable
  - Neuro-observations q1h (GCS, pupils, limb power) – escalate if drop ≥2 points
  - Maintain MAP >80 mmHg (to ensure cerebral perfusion)
  - Treat underlying cause: antibiotics for meningitis, dialysis for uraemia, removal of toxin, definitive management of stroke/ICH
  - Manage agitation or withdrawal syndromes (e.g. alcohol withdrawal – CIWA-Ar protocol)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Rehabilitation (speech, physio, OT)
  - Antiepileptics if seizure occurred
  - Neuropsychiatric follow-up if cognitive impairment persists
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride at 1 mL/kg/h (unless hyponatraemia – use isotonic fluids only)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) – **avoid** if ICH or high bleeding risk
  - **Diet/NBM:** keep NBM until GCS ≥15 and swallowing assessed (risk of aspiration)
  - **Observations:** neuro obs q1h + NEWS q30 min initially, then reduce if stable

### Drugs & Doses

- **Dextrose 50%:** 50 mL IV over 1‑2 min (for hypoglycaemia). Extravasation causes tissue necrosis – confirm IV patency.
- **Glucagon 1 mg IM** (if no IV access) – short duration; must give oral/IV glucose after.
- **Naloxone:** 400‑800 mcg IV, repeat up to 2 mg if no response. Rebound opioid toxicity (half-life 30-80 min) – monitor for recurrence.
- **Lorazepam:** 4 mg IV slow (over 1 min) – used for seizure. Avoid in severe respiratory depression.
- **Levetiracetam:** 1000‑2000 mg IV over 15 min (for status epilepticus) – [check local policy for exact dose].
- **Common alternatives:** Diazepam (rectal/IV), phenytoin (slower onset).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:** Naloxone may cause acute withdrawal (pain, vomiting, ventricular arrhythmias). Dextrose 50% contraindicated in hyperglycaemic hyperosmolar state (use normal saline instead).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Levetiracetam dose reduce in renal impairment (eGFR <50). Naloxone no adjustment. Dextrose no adjustment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - Giving naloxone 2 mg IV bolus in a rapid sequence can lead to pulmonary oedema and severe withdrawal. **Give small titrated doses (400 mcg) and reassess.**
  - Giving dextrose 50% without confirming hypoglycaemia in a stroke patient may worsen cerebral oedema. **Always check fingerstick first.**

### Complications

- **Acute:** aspiration pneumonitis, airway obstruction, raised ICP → herniation, seizures, cardiac arrythmias (tricyclic overdose), respiratory failure, respiratory paralysis (opioid overdose – naloxone wears off before opioid)
- **Chronic:** persistent cognitive deficit, post‑hypoxic myoclonus, epilepsy, contractures, pressure sores, DVT, C. diff colitis (if prolonged antibiotics)

### Prognosis

- **Hypoglycaemia:** complete recovery if treated within minutes of onset; prolonged (<1 hour) may cause permanent brain damage or death.
- **Structural lesion:** poor if fixed dilated pupils, extensor posturing, or GCS ≤4 on arrival (in‑hospital mortality >70% for ICH with GCS <8).
- **Metabolic/Toxic:** generally reversible with correction of underlying cause, except severe hypoxic‑ischaemic encephalopathy (e.g. post‑arrest).

### Pearls (positive actions)

- **DO check pupil reactivity first** – asymmetrical or non‑reacting pupils indicate structural pathology; symmetrical reactive pupils favour metabolic/toxic.
- **DO use AVPU as a rapid triage tool** – P (pain) or U (unresponsive) triggers immediate ABCDE.
- **DO get a collateral history** – from family, GP, ambulance crew: time of onset, medications, trauma, illicit drugs, alcohol, past medical history (especially diabetes, epilepsy, anticoagulation).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

- **DO NOT assume alcohol intoxication** without checking glucose, toxicology, and CT head – many structural lesions mimic intoxication.
- **DO NOT give naloxone rapidly or in large doses** – can cause acute withdrawal with hypertension, pulmonary oedema, and vomiting.
- **DO NOT forget non‑convulsive status epilepticus** in a persistent reduced GCS with no seizures – consider urgent EEG if GCS not improving.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

- **Return if:** GCS worsens despite reversal (e.g. hypoglycaemia – may recur due to insulin overdose); new focal weakness, headache, or confusion; seizure; vomiting.
- **Follow-up:** If cause was hypoglycaemia, ensure patient eats carbohydrate, check capillary glucose q15 min until stable >4.0 mmol/L.  Arrange blood glucose monitor + endocrine referral if unexplained. For overdose reversal, observe for 4-6 hours (naloxone short duration).

### Escalation Criteria

- **Call senior (reg / consultant) immediately if:**
  - GCS drops by ≥2 points
  - GCS ≤8 (consider intubation + ICU)
  - New focal neurology (hemiparesis, dysphasia, facial droop)
  - Signs of raised ICP (Cushing’s reflex)
  - Active seizure >5 minutes (status epilepticus)
  - National Early Warning Score (NEWS2) ≥5
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - Airway compromise (gag reflex absent, excessive secretions)
  - GCS ≤8 with no improvement after initial resuscitation
  - Respiratory failure (PaO2 <8 kPa on 60% O₂, PaCO2 >6.5 kPa)
  - Persistent tachyarrhythmia or cardiac arrest
